text: Narrative 1 - 'After takeoff [I] started after takeoff checklist. ATC instructed me to contact North Tower. I changed frequency before completing after takeoff checklist. During that time TCAS voice message [announced] 'traffic.' I got confused because TCAS is inoperative and placarded. I started right turn 2;150 feet MSL. ATC called me and gave me instruction for upwind. At that time; I was at 2;400 feet MSL; saw the aircraft left and below me. I told ATC 'aircraft in sight; climbing to avoid traffic.' ATC gave instructions to enter right downwind which I acknowledged. To avoid traffic I had climbed to 2;750 feet MSL then descended to TPA and landed safely.'
rationale: The pilot voluntarily abandoned the after-takeoff checklist midway to comply with an ATC frequency change, creating distraction that caused them to overlook the placarded, inoperative TCAS. When the disabled TCAS then announced 'traffic,' the pilot grew confused and initiated an unauthorized right turn without ATC clearance. The checklist and TCAS placard procedures were correct and present; it was the operator's divided attention and subsequent confusion—classic human factors—that drove the unsafe deviation.

text:
Narrative 1 - 'ATC vectors to ILS 19L. On base leg vector at 5000' we were given clearance to descend to 3100' on a heading of 160 degrees to intercept 19L localizer and once established cleared to descend to 2800' until SHAKE. We were a couple miles southwest of BERKS (15 DME ISIA) when given turn to intercept. At about 3100' just prior to LOC intercept we received a GPWS warning for terrain. I disconnected the autopilot and executed the GPWS escape maneuver. We climbed to about 5000' and continued to intercept the localizer and reloaded the approach. We notified ATC and once clear of the terrain warning and acquiring the runway; we continued the approach for a normal landing. We made visual contact with the airport just outside of SHAKE.'
Narrative 2 - '3100 feet near BERKS is approximately 1000 feet above the terrain but well below the 5000 foot altitude on the approach plate. We were surprised to get the warning but complied as we were night IMC. The whole event lasted just a few seconds.'
rationale: ATC vectored the crew to a descent altitude of 3,100 feet that was approximately 1,000 feet above the terrain but well below the 5,000-foot altitude charted on the published approach plate, triggering a GPWS terrain warning on a night IMC approach. The crew followed every ATC instruction correctly and did not deviate from the clearance, yet still received an unexpected terrain alert. This is a procedure error because the ATC vectoring practice created an unsafe descent profile that conflicted with published approach constraints, not a failure of the crew to execute their duties.

text: Narrative 1 - 'While assisting the Terminal Arrival Radar (TAR)-D Controller Aircraft X came over 'descending via' the WINNG Optimized Profile Descent (OPD) arrival. The controller did not adjust Aircraft X's speed; but the aircraft still was not able to make the crossing restrictions further along the arrival; resulting in two different airspace violations. This is a common problem; and it is greatly exaggerated when aircraft are slowed below published speeds. Many times it is not appropriate to feed the Final Controller at speeds above 210 knots; and slowing to this speed almost always results in aircraft not being able to make crossing restrictions and violating satellite and/or departure airspace.Terminate OPD arrivals with hard altitudes and appropriate speeds at the A80 airspace boundary. The efficiencies gained by OPD 'descend via' arrivals within the A80 airspace is not worth the added risk and complexity. The procedures as they stand today are unsafe. A deviation into satellite airspace is unavoidable and if satellite were to have a plane in their high corridor (they only have 1 altitude - 11;000 feet) there is nowhere to go.'
rationale: The WINNG OPD 'descend via' arrival's crossing restrictions were structurally incompatible with the speed reductions ATC routinely imposed for final feeding, resulting in repeated and predictable airspace violations. The reporter explicitly states 'this is a common problem' and 'the procedures as they stand today are unsafe,' confirming the issue was systemic and not tied to any single controller's error. The documented procedure was fundamentally flawed: controllers following it correctly still produced unsafe outcomes, making this a procedure error rather than a human performance failure.

text: Narrative 1 - 'Forwarding Internal Safety Report file by flight crew of Aircraft X after landing: 'We were working with arrival and told we would be #1 into 07FA. An Aircraft Y was approaching from the north and told he was number 2 and to slow down. He canceled the IFR and immediately left the Approach frequency. We were told to keep our speed up because we would still be number one. As we approached; the arrival controller called us several times to call out traffic at what time it appeared we would have a conflict. Once visual we called a 6 mile final the Aircraft Y then informed us he was cutting in front of us in a 2 mile base. He flat out cut us off. We slowed the best we could but at 800 ft. it was clear we wouldn't be able land as they still had to backtrack off the runway. We imitated a go around and landed uneventfully. The UNICOM was flabbergasted at what had taken place; I never ended up talking to the crew of the other plane.''
rationale: After being sequenced as number two, the pilot of Aircraft Y unilaterally canceled IFR and immediately abandoned the approach frequency without coordinating with ATC or the preceding aircraft, then cut in front of Aircraft X on a two-mile base. This reflects a breakdown in communication and situational awareness by the conflicting pilot, who disregarded standard sequencing etiquette and made no radio calls announcing his repositioning. The separation and sequencing procedures existed and were correctly applied by ATC; the hazard arose entirely from one operator's deliberate and uncoordinated action.

text: Narrative 1 - '55 gal drums full of chemicals are not being stored properly inside Hangar Bay X. The drums are sitting on regular wood pallets instead of spill proof platforms per our SOP.The drums appear to be leaking and fluid is going into the floor drain. I have made several local attempts through 'closing the loop' to get the situation corrected with no luck.'
rationale: Ground personnel storing chemicals in Hangar Bay X used regular wood pallets in place of the SOP-mandated spill-proof platforms, allowing leaking drums to drain into the floor drain despite repeated corrective attempts by the reporter. The SOP was clearly established and required the correct equipment, yet the responsible personnel persistently failed to comply. This is a human factors issue—specifically involving non-aviation ground crew who repeatedly ignored documented safety procedures—rather than any flaw in the SOP itself.

text: Narrative 1 - 'I flew into ZZZ in VMC conditions; landed on Runway XX and taxied back on the parallel runway towards the ramp. It was shortly after noon; weather was clear; with a significant crosswind.  There was not a lot of traffic so after landing tower advised me to 'taxi to the ramp and stay with me' which I read back and acknowledged. While taxiing back I noticed a Cherokee 6 on final. There was a fair amount of cross wind (approximately 20 knots crosswind component). The Cherokee called tower and asked 'Am I still cleared to land?' That prompted me to look up and visually see the airplane was clearly heading towards me on the taxiway and lined up to land on the taxiway. The tower replied to the Cherokee and said 'yes; cleared to land'. At that time; I immediately keyed the mic and told the Cherokee to go around. I stated 'Go around; go around; you are lined up on the taxiway; go around. The pilot initiated the go around and the tower then also instructed the pilot to go around at about the same time the pilot had initiated the go around. After a few moments the pilot radioed an apology and said 'Okay I see what I did now; sorry'. I would estimate the separation at about 50-75 feet when the pilot went around. If we had not all been on tower frequency; this could have ended much differently.'
rationale: The Cherokee 6 pilot, distracted or disoriented by a significant crosswind, lined up on the occupied taxiway instead of the runway and asked tower 'Am I still cleared to land?' while already on approach to the wrong surface. Despite clear VMC conditions, active tower communications, and a visible aircraft on the taxiway, the pilot failed to perceive the error until another pilot and ATC intervened with go-around instructions. All required procedures and markings were in place; the incident was driven entirely by the inbound pilot's loss of situational awareness during the approach.

text: Narrative 1 - 'I did not fully understand that the 400 feet AGL rule applies to all drones. I also believed that my drone was under 0.55 lbs; since this is how it was advertised online on the page from which I purchased my specific configuration.'
rationale: The drone operator was unaware that the 400-foot AGL altitude restriction applies to all drones regardless of weight, and also held an incorrect belief about the aircraft's weight class based on an online product listing rather than official documentation. Both failures reflect a deficit in regulatory knowledge and pre-flight qualification that enabled an inadvertent airspace violation. The regulations were clear and publicly available; the root cause was the operator's insufficient training and understanding of the applicable rules.

text: Narrative 1 - 'Runway at SGJ is 13/31. Traffic was departing from 31 on Day 0. Received Ground instructions to taxi and hold at B6; which is last taxiway prior to 31. Received subsequent instructions to hold at Runway 31 and switch to Tower. NOTAM for airport lists 'runway markings faded'. Pilot mistook the instructions to 'hold at 31' and misinterpreted lines between Taxiway B6 and Runway 31; and proceeded past hold for Taxiway B6 (which is also the hold for Runway 31) to edge of Runway 31. After switching to Tower; Tower informed pilot that he had proceeded too close to runway without permission and approaching aircraft went around. Proximity of aircraft was not within about 500 linear feet; but was technically a runway incursion although aircraft was not on Runway 31. The runway markings; instructions; and taxiway positions for entering Runway 31 at SGJ are confusing without local knowledge and the NOTAM added to the confusion. Suggest that SGJ Ground replace 'hold at Runway 31 and switch to Tower' to 'hold at B6 and switch to Tower' to avoid confusion in the future for Runway 31.'
rationale: Despite an active NOTAM warning of faded runway markings and a ground instruction to 'hold at B6'—the hold-short point for Runway 31—the pilot misinterpreted the subsequent instruction to 'hold at Runway 31' and advanced past the hold-short marking onto the runway environment, causing an approaching aircraft to go around. The correct hold-short point, the NOTAM, and the ATC instruction were all present and correct; the error stemmed from the pilot's misinterpretation of standard phraseology and failure to reconcile the conflicting spatial cues in the degraded marking environment.

text: Narrative 1 - 'The flight plan of Aircraft X was passed to Approach (non radar adjacent facility that controls surface-10;000 ft). The estimated time over NLD VOR that was passed was XA:47z at 8000 feet. At some point a flight plan was copied from Approach regarding a LRD lander. Because Approach requires non-radar separation they would need to de-conflict any conflicts through their airspace. Aircraft Y populated on my radar scope when he was descending from 11;500 feet to 5;000 feet. At the time; I did not have this flight plan in my strip bay. Upon noticing the conflict I immediately called Approach to make sure they were stopping the traffic at 9;000 feet above my coordinated overflight at 8;000 feet. By the time I got a hold of the Approach Controller and issued him a control action of maintaining 9;000 feet the Aircraft Y was out of 8;700 feet in the climb back up to 9;000 with 8 miles lateral. Furthermore; I must mention that not having a direct and quick line to Approach is a safety hazard. We must manually dial a 7 digit number and wait for a response; to then be passed over to the Approach Controller. We must have a direct and quick line that allows us to speak to an adjacent facility. Approach was applying non radar separation to aircraft transitioning through there airspace.'
rationale: The controller could not reach Approach Control quickly enough during an emerging conflict because the only available communication path required manually dialing a seven-digit number and waiting to be transferred, introducing a critical delay when immediate coordination was required. This procedural gap—the absence of a direct hotline between adjacent facilities—was the structural cause of the near-miss, not any lapse in individual controller judgment. If a direct line had been in place as a procedural standard, the controller's timely awareness and action would have resolved the conflict before it developed.

text: Narrative 1 - 'Light aircraft; 270 heading off Runway XXC climbing out of 6;000 at 3.8 feet per minute climb rate we received a TA/RA. Instructed to level off. We had traffic in sight and complied with the level off. Cleared conflict with about 1;000 feet plus separation and VFR horizontal separation.Light aircraft high climb rate.Reduction of throttle and lower pitch and complied with RA.'
rationale: The aircraft was climbing out at an abnormally low rate of 3.8 feet per minute, an aerodynamic performance failure that generated a TCAS TA/RA with conflicting traffic. The crew complied with the RA level-off instruction and resolved the conflict; however, the triggering event was the pilot's failure to maintain a standard climb profile that would have kept adequate vertical separation. All TCAS and ATC procedures functioned correctly; the root cause was the human operator's failure to monitor and correct the aircraft's dangerously degraded climb performance.

text: Narrative 1 - 'Departed VFR out of Teterboro; proceeded VFR below bravo shelves towards Alpine Tower. Traffic was pointed out to us 12 o'clock 200 ft. higher than our altitude. Continued to look for traffic. ATC gave us an immediate right turn east bound. Proceeded to get RA alert telling us to descend. After the initial descent traffic was spotted at our 10 o'clock. Altitude descended as around 100 ft. Immediately received a GPWS terrain/pull up alert; all terrain was being visually avoided. During that alert we received a bravo clearance; turn to 360 as well as a climb to 13;500 ft. The heading was missed in our acknowledgement due to pilot workload. Continued on original heading for maybe 15 seconds before ATC returned with a new heading.'
rationale: Flying VFR in congested airspace near Teterboro, the crew received simultaneous and cascading alerts—a TCAS RA, a GPWS terrain warning, a bravo airspace clearance, and a heading change—that overwhelmed their cognitive capacity, causing them to miss and not acknowledge the ATC heading instruction for approximately 15 seconds. The crew's workload saturation in a high-density, complex environment led to task shedding under time pressure, a textbook human factors failure. The procedures (TCAS, GPWS, ATC coordination) all operated correctly; the breakdown occurred in the crew's ability to process and action all inputs simultaneously.

text: Narrative 1 - 'As the Tower Controller In Charge I was notified that there was an inbound emergency. I call the ARFF (our fire/rescue/emergency line) from the Tower and passed all emergency information.First fire truck calls ground for permission to set up staging and was given instructions during heavy ground complexity traffic. Then another firetruck calls for further permission to move and stage. The Ground Controller had to clarify who was calling due to them possibly using/saying the wrong call sign along with a lot of ground traffic. This caused a lot of confusion and the Ground Controller had to do a lot of deciphering to ascertain who was who. Then a fire truck asked to cross Runway 20R and the Ground Controller gives permission to cross and the vehicle never crosses the runway and we (the controllers in the Tower cab) don't know if the runway is clear or not! Then Airport Command calls and wants to get permission to stage and follow the emergency aircraft.Per our SOP and agreement with the Fire Station; Airport Command is the only person who is supposed to communicate with Ground Control and coordinate the movement of emergency response vehicles and personnel. The fire trucks got confused; call signs got tangled; while all along Ground Control was not even supposed to be talking to any of the fire trucks; only Airport Command.I talked to the Fire Chief in charge of the situation and he said; and I quote; since I wrote down his responses:'We are training dispatchers and they didn't do what they are suppose too''The trucks didn't know I was behind them; sorry''It was a complete flub job on our part''It was not our best effort'This issue has been addressed and addressed by supervisors up the command chain and supposedly to the Fire Department.Nothing has changed.I have personally witnessed the same fiasco multiple times and go to my management expecting results since this is a safety issue.This is completely unsafe and the Ground Controller had way too much on his 'plate' especially all along having to deal with an issue that should be very clean; cut; dry and simple. So here are my recommendations.-Enforce the procedures with disciplinary action. Not briefings after another incident.-Give the controllers the ability to enforce the rules. Controllers will not authorize a Fire Truck to do anything. Airport Command will only be communicated with.-Come up with a pass/fail written test on the procedures.-Come up with a pass/fail On the Job Training (OJT) performance evaluation on procedures.-Arrange tours for both controllers and fire truck operators so that we can see what they do; and they can see what we do.I am not saying I know exactly what to do. But the next time a Fire Truck calls and they start screwing up the Ground Frequency someone is going to hurt or even worse; killed. This needs to be solved ASAP.'
rationale: The emergency response SOP clearly designated Airport Command as the sole point of contact with Ground Control for vehicle movements, yet fire trucks repeatedly bypassed Airport Command and called Ground Control directly, using incorrect call signs and fragmenting the controller's attention during a high-complexity emergency. The Fire Chief himself acknowledged the department's dispatchers failed to follow the established procedure, confirming that the protocol itself was sound. This is a procedure error in the systemic sense: the coordination agreement existed but lacked enforcement mechanisms, training standards, and accountability measures that would make compliance reliable—turning a correct procedure into one that was functionally unsafe.

text: Narrative 1 - 'Incident happened during the missed approach for AST ILS26.  I mis-read the altitude for the hold portion of the missed approach as 2000 when in fact it is 2900. The mis-read happened when briefing the approach and again while initiating the climb out.  Center issued an altitude alert while I was in the hold and I immediately climbed.  The error was unintentional.  The initial straight ahead part of the MAP was a climb to 1500 and I remember thinking that finishing the climb to only 2000 seemed low. To prevent this from happening again in the future I will be double checking missed approach altitudes with the chart magnified sufficiently to prevent mis-reads.'
rationale: The pilot misread the missed approach holding altitude as 2,000 feet when it was clearly published as 2,900 feet, and this same misread occurred twice—once during the approach brief and again during execution—before an ATC altitude alert prompted immediate correction. The correct altitude was on the chart and available for cross-check; the error was a purely cognitive one, a failure to accurately perceive printed information that the pilot even noted 'seemed low.' This is a human factors error of perception and insufficient verification, not any ambiguity or flaw in the published approach procedure.

text: Narrative 1 - 'Enroute from ZZZ to ZZZ1 on an IFR clearance at FL330. Approximately 5 minutes from ZZZZZ; the Center Controller attempted to issue a revised clearance to fly the arrival. I did not accept the clearance as the aircraft I was operating can not comply with the note on the arrival clearly stating the aircraft must be capable of 280 kts or greater. The maximum speed in Aircraft X is 250 kts. Notes on published charts are legally binding for the pilot. I requested to stay on the arrival as this is a legitimate alternative to the STAR for ZZZ arrivals. The controller then threatened me with an indefinite hold until I chose to comply with his clearance to do the STAR which I can not legally accept. He finaly decided to clear me for the arrival which has a note saying GPS equipped turbojets must file the STAR.I realize that the problem here is the ridiculous and ambiguous Arrival procedures of ZZZ TRACON. That is another issue. My safety concern here is that an FAA Air Traffic Controller attempted to extort me with indefinite delay and possible fuel starvation if I did not accept and comply with a clearance I could not legally accept.'
rationale: An ATC controller attempted to issue a STAR clearance that the aircraft could not legally accept due to a published speed requirement exceeding its performance capability, then threatened the pilot with an indefinite hold as coercion when the pilot correctly refused. The controller's failure to find a lawful alternative clearance and use of delay as leverage created a genuine safety hazard—potential fuel starvation—through a misuse of authority and communication breakdown. The published arrival procedures, while noted as ambiguous, were not the proximate cause; the immediate threat arose from the individual controller's conduct and decision-making.

text: Narrative 1 - 'We were transitioning in VFR conditions on the ZZZZZ3 arrival to the GPS approach to XXR. We loaded the approach into the FMS and set bottom attitude for the approach. I was in right seat flying and CA was in the left who is a CE510s rated pilot also. I was off auto pilot and hand flying with a different flight director display than normally used. The issue was when engaged the appr mode on flight director while still in Vnav instead of waiting until the GPS switched to LPV mode just outside of the final approach fix. The controller advised us that we had descended below the minimum vectoring altitude for that segment of the approach. The clear message here was that better crew coordination was needed.'
rationale: While hand-flying an approach with an unfamiliar flight director display, the first officer prematurely engaged approach mode while still in VNAV—before the GPS transitioned to LPV mode at the final approach fix—causing the aircraft to descend below minimum vectoring altitude. The report itself identifies improved crew resource management as the corrective action, and the CA, also a qualified CE510 pilot, did not intervene before the descent violation. The FMS, approach procedure, and published minimums were all correct; the error was an improper FMS mode sequencing by the operator under inadequate crew coordination.

text: Narrative 1 - 'I was training a developmental. We had a radar assist with little experience. The radar controller at sector 33 has very little experience and they were training on his assist side at d-33. Sec 32's airspace starts at 34000 feet. D-33 flashes Aircraft X to us out of 150 climbing and somehow either my developmental or our d-side takes the handoff that early. After a couple minutes the radar controller at sector 33 says he would just keep Aircraft X because he was climbing so slow and we just accepted a point out climbing to 35000 feet. We never flashed Aircraft X back to sector 33. Sector 33 climbs Aircraft X to 35000 feet and after a few minutes ships him to ZLC 44 because he thought they had radar contact with him. Neither me; my trainee; nor assist controller realized we still had control of the data block. Aircraft X then starts to conflict alert flash with a LAS arrival coming from ZLC 44 at 32000 feet. Aircraft X was still climbing out of 28000 feet and the data block still reflected he was climbing to 35000 feet right into the LAS arrival. Not I; the developmental; or the assist questioned the radar controller at sector 33; because we all assumed that sector 33 was taking care of it. After Aircraft X was 4 miles into ZLC 44's airspace and still climbing out of 29000 feet; ZLC 44 called our assist and said that they were talking to Aircraft X and they stopped him below the LAS arrival. There were a lot of things that went wrong in this situation. Lack of experience has a lot to do with this. I should have realized that we still had control of the data block and had my developmental either handoff back to sector 33 or handoff to ZLC 44. I was always trained not to assume anything in this job. I assumed that sector 33 was taking care of traffic in their airspace without questioning. The auto handoff flash must not have worked to ZLC 44 or this wouldn't have happened. ZLC 44 should have called sector 33 when Aircraft X checked on their frequency or sent him back to sector 33 and none of this would have happened. There was a lack of teamwork everywhere and it all could have been avoided.'
rationale: When Aircraft X's data block was prematurely accepted by one sector during a handoff, no controller across three sectors—the trainer, the developmental, or the assist—questioned who held control, and the automated handoff flash to ZLC 44 failed without triggering any procedural backup to verify the transfer. The controller's own analysis notes that neither the auto-handoff system nor the inter-facility coordination protocols provided a reliable mechanism to detect the orphaned data block before a conflict alert developed. This is a procedure error because the automated coordination system failed and no formal manual backup procedure existed to catch or verify missed inter-facility handoffs.

text: Narrative 1 - 'The aircraft came into contact with an unoccupied parked aircraft while taxiing; but there were no injuries and to the best of my knowledge no substantial damage.'
rationale: During taxi, the aircraft made contact with an unoccupied, stationary parked aircraft, an event with no procedural ambiguity involved—the pilot was required to maintain clearance from all obstacles during ground operations. The incident reflects a straightforward lapse in situational awareness on the part of the taxiing pilot, who failed to adequately perceive and account for the proximity of the parked aircraft. No procedure was flawed or absent; the sole cause was the human operator's failure to safely navigate a static ground environment.

text: Narrative 1 - 'On approach into ZZZ; center warned me that there was traffic near the airport that he wasn't talking to. I canceled IFR a little early as it was a clear day and I was familiar with the area; and I had a light transport inbound behind me I didn't want to hold up. I started my calls at 20 miles from the field and called my position and intentions every 2-3 miles on the way in. I was aiming for entry to the left downwind to 13 on a straight-in entry to the pattern at pattern altitude. At about 25 miles from the field; Cessna 182 called that he was overflying runway 31 at 2500 feet. This was the only radio call they made; and due to the fact that I've had issue with Aircraft Y pilot not making radio calls I made the decision to exercise my Pilot in Command rights to safely complete the flight and used my personal ForeFlight subscription on my phone to verify where the traffic was. I was on autopilot; and my phone was already hooked to the onboard ADSB for exactly these cases; and I left it on the seat next to me in clear view to create no distractions. At 7 miles from the field I called Aircraft Y pilot after I had made another position report and had heard nothing from him. I asked him his intentions and he said he was headed north from the airport at 5 miles and would be circling back around to overfly runway 31 again. As there was potential conflict with the final to 13; I wanted to make sure we were clear on expectations on where my aircraft and his were heading. As I entered the downwind; I looked one last time at the traffic report on my phone and saw that he was headed away from the airport; about 5 miles west; northwest. As I made my turn to base and my call; I received a traffic alert from the airplane that traffic was two miles; same altitude. I looked again at ADSB and saw him on a direct heading for the runway and at my altitude. I demanded over radio that he make an immediate turn away from my aircraft as I was about to turn final and he should have been able to de-conflict more easily. He told me that he had me in sight and was going to head for the runway to overfly it. At this point I began to put in landing flaps and increase the descent rate towards the runway. I am very comfortable with the airport environment there and the aircraft performance and made the decision to make a faster; steeper approach to get out of the pathway of the Aircraft Y as he was an unknown with heading and altitude other than he was heading for the runway and I couldn't see him. At this point my phone showed him at half a mile from my plane and closing at the same altitude so I was reacting quickly. I again demanded he turn away from my aircraft as he was endangering our lives and he wasn't making radio calls and he didn't respond. I landed safely and taxied off the ramp and the Aircraft Y was directly overhead on my rollout. He made no other radio calls and I didn't see him at all until after I landed.I identified [conflict traffic] on base turn when I received the traffic alert.[Reporter stated] conflict aircraft was flying unsafely and not making radio calls. I can't control the other pilot; but it is immensely frustrating to fly high-performance turboprop aircraft into uncontrolled fields in 2020 with equipment available that could make the flight safer but not installed in my aircraft. Beside all other obvious benefits of GPS equipment with ADSB displays; a generic ADSB callout with no way to display the position of traffic is far more distracting than it should be. I should not have to make the decision to break company policy to ensure my safety and a successful flight when other pilots are acting erratically. Had I not had my phone out with the traffic displayed; I might've made different choices with a poorer outcome. I highly recommend that the company reweigh the benefits of waiting to standardize GPS equipment across the fleet.'
rationale: The primary hazard was created by a conflict pilot who failed to make required radio calls, flew an unpredictable pattern, and refused to yield despite direct radio demands, exhibiting poor communication and situational awareness in an uncontrolled environment. The reporting pilot followed all correct procedures—canceling IFR, making position calls every 2-3 miles, monitoring ADSB, and demanding the conflict aircraft turn away—yet was still placed in a near-collision scenario by the other pilot's conduct. The procedures and self-separation tools were all functioning; the incident was caused entirely by one human operator's failure to communicate and maintain safe situational awareness.

text: Narrative 1 - 'After parking a fully loaded -800; I went to the ramp to do a walk around for our next flight. As I approached the nose wheel well; I immediately noticed that I did not have to bend over to enter the wheel well. The nose scissors and strut were fully extended. I have never seen that on a 737 and first thought it was an over serviced strut. I continued my walk around while thinking over the nose issue. As I returned to the nose I saw the strut actually compress to a normal position. At this point the Operations Lead came running down the stairs after he immediately stopped passengers from deplaning. He then told me the ramp around [the gate] has a sloping grade rearward. He also said that because of that slope; another carrier actually had a -800 that had [the nose wheel] come off the ground the week before. I found this valuable safety information.'
rationale: The ramp at this gate had a documented rearward slope known to cause nose strut extension on 737-800 aircraft—so well known that another carrier had experienced a nose wheel departure at the same gate the prior week—yet this hazard had not been incorporated into gate assignment procedures, parking checklists, or any formal notification to line crews. The pilot discovered the anomaly during a walkaround, not through any procedural alert, and only learned the cause from the Operations Lead after the fact. This is a procedure error because a known, recurring physical hazard at a specific gate was not mitigated or documented in any formal operational procedure.

text: Narrative 1 - 'Smart bag was checked in. Lithium battery still connected. It was stopped here in Denver and the battery was removed.'
rationale: The smart bag with its lithium battery still connected passed through check-in at the origin airport without being flagged, only being intercepted at Denver—indicating the check-in procedure at the origin failed to screen for or enforce the requirement to remove or disconnect lithium batteries from smart bags before acceptance as checked luggage. If the screening procedure had been effective, the non-compliant bag would not have been accepted. This is a procedure error because the existing check-in process was insufficient to reliably identify and reject bags with active lithium battery connections before they entered the baggage system.
